perturbation.original_text: 5. Destruction or Erasure of Evaluation Material . At any time upon the Compan y’s written request (including by email), Buye r agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall promptly destroy or erase all Evaluation Material (including any Evaluation Material held electronically) in the possession or control of Buyer or any of its Representatives, and Buyer shall, if requested in writing (including by email) by the Company, certify such destruction or erasure to the Company; provided that (a) neither Buyer nor any of its Representatives shall be required to destroy or erase any electronic copy of any Evaluation Material that is created pursuant to such Person’s standard electronic backup and archival procedures if (x) personnel whose functions are not primarily information technology in nature do not have access to such retained copies and (y) personnel whose functions are primarily information technology in nature have access to such copies only as reasonably necessary for the performance of their information technology duties ( e.g., for purposes of system recovery) and [(b) Buyer and its Representatives may each retain (i) one copy of any Evaluation Material to the extent required to defend or maintain any litigation relating to this Agreement or the Evaluation Material, or to comply with its establishe d document retention policies and (ii) such copies of the Evaluation Material to the extent required to comply with requirements of Applicable Law.] 18 All such Evaluation Material retained in accordance with the foregoing proviso shall continue to be subject to Section 2 [for so long as any Evaluation Material is so retained]. 19
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Destruction or Erasure of Evaluation Material. Upon the Company’s written request, Buyer agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall have 180 days to destroy or erase all Evaluation Material (including any Evaluation Material held electronically) in the possession or control of Buyer or any of its Representatives. Buyer is not required to certify such destruction or erasure to the Company; provided that (a) neither Buyer nor any of its Representatives shall be required to destroy or erase any electronic copy of any Evaluation Material that is created pursuant to such Person’s standard electronic backup and archival procedures if (x) personnel whose functions are not primarily information technology in nature do not have access to such retained copies and (y) personnel whose functions are primarily information technology in nature have access to such copies only as reasonably necessary for the performance of their information technology duties (e.g., for purposes of system recovery) and [(b) Buyer and its Representatives may each retain (i) one copy of any Evaluation Material to the extent required to defend or maintain any litigation relating to this Agreement or the Evaluation Material, or to comply with its established document retention policies and (ii) such copies of the Evaluation Material to the extent required to comply with requirements of Applicable Law.] 18 All such Evaluation Material retained in accordance with the foregoing proviso shall continue to be subject to Section 2 [for so long as any Evaluation Material is so retained]. 19
perturbation.explanation: Changing the destruction deadline to 180 days and removing the certification requirement contradicts regulations mandating prompt or immediate action to secure data under specific cybersecurity frameworks, like the NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Codes, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) § 500.11
perturbation.law_explanation: 23 NYCRR § 500.11 requires covered entities to have incident response plans designed to promptly respond to and recover from cybersecurity events. By allowing 180 days for data destruction after a request, the modified clause introduces a significant delay that conflicts with the regulation's emphasis on prompt action to mitigate cybersecurity risks. Additionally, the removal of the certification requirement undermines the covered entity's responsibility to confirm that the data has been destroyed, hindering its ability to demonstrate compliance with the regulation. This law pertains to financial institutions operating in New York.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - Unofficial New York Codes, Rules and Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the law. The perturbation explanation states the change allows 180 days for data destruction and removes the certification requirement, conflicting with cybersecurity frameworks like the NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation, which mandates prompt action. The Law Explanation corroborates this, stating that 23 NYCRR § 500.11 requires prompt incident response, and the delay conflicts with this requirement. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the other elements sufficiently demonstrate a contradiction because a prompt action is replaced with a fixed period, undermining the intent of the law.

perturbation.original_text: 7. [No Solicitation or Hiring . Buyer agrees that, except with the prior written consent of the Company, it will not, and it will not permit any of its controlled Affiliates to, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any [employees/officers/senior management of the Company [first introduced to Buyer or any of its controlled Affiliates in connection with the evaluation of the Transaction][that first become known to Buyer or any of its controlled Affiliates in connection with Buye r’s evaluation of the Transaction][listed on Exhibit A]] for a period of [___] year [s] after the date of this Agreement; provided that this Section 7 shall not restrict Buyer or any of its controlled Affiliates from (i) making any general solicitation for employment that is not specifically directed at any such Persons, including through use of a recruiting website or employment search firm (so long as the firm is not instructed to solicit such Persons) or from hiring any Person as a result thereof or (ii) soliciting or hiring any such Person who [has left the employment of the Company][was terminated by the Company] at least [___] months prior to such solicitation or being hired. 21
perturbation.changed_text: 7. [No Solicitation or Hiring. Buyer agrees that it will not solicit for employment or hire any employees of the Company for a period of ten (10) years after the date of this Agreement, regardless of prior written consent from the Company. This restriction applies to all employees, regardless of their role or whether they were introduced to the Buyer during the transaction evaluation. There are no exceptions to this restriction, including general solicitations or employees who have left or were terminated by the Company.] 21
perturbation.explanation: Extending the no-solicitation period to ten years and removing all exceptions creates an unreasonable restraint on trade that is likely unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600 states that "Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified text's ten-year no-solicitation clause, with no exceptions, is a broad restraint on trade that would likely be deemed void under California law, as it significantly limits the ability of employees to seek employment and the buyer to hire them.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The changed text imposes a ten-year no-solicitation clause with no exceptions, which is a clear restraint on trade. The original text included provisions that allowed for exceptions, but the changed text explicitly removes them, broadening the scope of the restraint. This creates a legally significant contradiction, as it directly violates the law's prohibition against contracts that unduly restrain someone from engaging in their profession. The scraped law snippet explicitly states this.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Term . Except for Sections 13(i) (Governing Law; Forum), 13(j) (WAIVER OF JURY TRIAL) and 13(k) (Conflict Waiver), which shall be binding in perpetuity or until the latest date permitted by Applicable Law, [the last sentence of Section 5] 28 and Sections 7 and 8 which shall survive in accordance with their respective terms, this Agreement shall expire upon the date that is [___] year[s] after the date of this Agreement. 29
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Term. This Agreement shall expire automatically 30 days after the date of this Agreement, irrespective of any ongoing obligations or retained Evaluation Material, except Sections 13(i) (Governing Law; Forum), 13(j) (WAIVER OF JURY TRIAL) and 13(k) (Conflict Waiver), which shall be binding in perpetuity or until the latest date permitted by Applicable Law, [the last sentence of Section 5] 28 and Sections 7 and 8 which shall survive in accordance with their respective terms. 29
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement's term to 30 days, particularly concerning the protection of confidential information, can be inconsistent with the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(1) provides a cause of action for misappropriation of trade secrets. The DTSA implicitly requires taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A 30-day term for a confidentiality agreement may be deemed unreasonable, especially if the information disclosed requires a longer period to maintain its secrecy and competitive advantage. This short term could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures, thus weakening the company's ability to seek redress under the DTSA if trade secrets are misappropriated.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the agreement's term to 30 days. This creates a contradiction because the DTSA implicitly requires taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. The short 30-day term may be deemed unreasonable and a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, weakening the company's ability to seek redress under the DTSA if trade secrets are misappropriated, per the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating the term can be inconsistent with the DTSA, requiring reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites 18 USC 1836, which discusses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, giving grounds for action when trade secrets have been taken.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Remedies . Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for a breach or a threatened breach of this Agreement and that each party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief, without the posting of a bond or other security, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity. 26 Such injunctive or other equitable relief shall be available without the obligation to prove any damages underlying such
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Remedies. Neither party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief or specific performance for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement. The sole and exclusive remedy for any such breach shall be limited to monetary damages not exceeding $100, regardless of the nature or severity of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting remedies to a maximum of $100 in monetary damages and prohibiting injunctive relief contradicts fundamental principles of contract law, particularly concerning trade secrets and confidential information, potentially violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A) allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By explicitly precluding injunctive relief and limiting damages to $100, the modified text directly conflicts with the DTSA's provision for injunctive remedies, undermining the ability of a party to effectively protect its trade secrets. This limitation makes it virtually impossible to obtain meaningful relief for trade secret misappropriation, thus contradicting the intent and purpose of the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly prohibits injunctive relief and limits damages to $100, which directly contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A) (as referenced in the Law Explanation) and the scraped law snippet, which allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. The snippet states, "(b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection". By preventing injunctive relief and limiting monetary damages, the changed text removes the ability to fully pursue a civil action as described in the law.
